artifacts.content:
Hi Ana,

Catching up between supervision hours while the students are watching a video in LL-21. I owe you a long update.

Work first: things at the Psychological Services Clinic have felt heavier again this semester. Do you remember that near-hospitalization back in late 2024 in LL-18 with the suicidal UH student? The one who walked out saying he didn’t know why he kept going, and then casually mentioned he had a handgun in his car parked just outside the Health and Behavioral Sciences Center? My senior trainee and I both felt physically unsafe for the first time in a long while. That’s what really pushed me over the edge about firming up the carry exclusion zone in the HBSC basement and first floor.

It hasn’t just been that. In LL-25 we’ve got this high-conflict custody case where the dad has a documented history of brandishing a gun during a domestic fight. Every time those parents come through LL-W2 I’m on edge, imagining what would happen if he were legally carrying on campus and something escalated in the waiting area.

All of this is why I finally started seeing a therapist myself near the Texas Medical Center back in mid-2023. I told you a little, but not the details. After the third near-hospitalization in those LL-10–24 rooms, and that community client at HBSC 110 who threatened to “come back with a gun,” I just couldn’t sleep. Weekly 50 minutes of being the patient instead of the psychologist has been…humbling, but it’s the only reason I’m not completely burned out.

On the home front, kids are fine—Elena is obsessed with her piano recital and Mateo wants to be a goalie now. The bigger worry is Mami and Papi. Dad’s orthopedist in Sugar Land told him early this year he really shouldn’t be driving into Houston anymore because of his knees and reaction time. He’s ignoring half of it, of course, but it makes me more anxious about any campus crisis, knowing they couldn’t just hop in the car and be here.

We should really plan that San Antonio/Big Bend trip for next summer. I need something to look forward to.

Besos,
Melissa

Sunday planning – HBSC / home / my own sanity

1. Clinic safety + exclusion zone
- Keep pushing gently on the HBSC exclusion zone for the Psychological Services Clinic (LL-10–32, LL-W1/W2, HBSC 110–116). I keep replaying that LL-18 session from late 2024: suicidal student, walked out saying “I don’t know why I keep going,” then on the way out mentions he has a handgun in his car outside HBSC. Senior trainee shaking afterward. That’s exactly the scenario I’m trying to prevent.
- Same with the LL-25 custody case: dad with prior incident of brandishing a firearm in a domestic dispute. LL-W2 feels like a powder keg when both parents are in the waiting area.
- Remember the 2023 community client at HBSC 110 reception who said he’d “come back with a gun.” I ended up calling my contact at UHPD off the record to ask what authority I had to ask someone to leave or request a welfare check without triggering a full incident report. I do NOT want to be improvising next time.
- Trainee well-being: that 2024 supervision note about the second-year who broke down after a crisis session in LL-21 and confessed they’d started carrying a concealed handgun off-campus just to feel safe walking to their car after late nights in HBSC. If the *trainees* feel that unsafe, we have to change the environment.

2. My own support
- Keep weekly therapy near the Med Center. Burnout/anxiety around repeated crises (LL-10–24) is real, even if I’m functioning. Don’t cancel to make room for “one more meeting.”
- Talk honestly with [CAPS colleague] at Friday lunch about how much of this is sustainable.

3. Family logistics
- Check in with Ana about Dad’s orthopedist telling him not to drive into Houston anymore. Start the “what if you couldn’t stay in Sugar Land?” conversation with Mami and Papi.
- Block calendar for Elena’s recital, Mateo’s soccer games, and one totally protected family weekend a month.

4. Longer-term
- If the exclusion zone signage goes up (HBSC 110 entrance, LL elevator lobby, etc.), make sure bilingual wording doesn’t inadvertently single out the Latinx families who are already terrified of gun-related retaliation.
- Revisit the Latinx trauma project when I’m not constantly in crisis mode.

Hi [Name],

Following up on our hallway conversation about campus safety around the Psychological Services Clinic in HBSC.

I want you to understand why I’ve become so adamant about clearly designating the clinic footprint (LL-10 through LL-32, LL-W1/W2, and HBSC 110–116) as a firearm exclusion zone and making sure signage at HBSC 110 and the lower-level elevator lobby is unambiguous.

In late 2024 we had a crisis session in LL-18 with a UH student who met criteria for near-hospitalization. As he was leaving, after an hour of talking about not wanting to live, he mentioned having a handgun in his car parked just outside the Health and Behavioral Sciences Center. My senior trainee and I both felt physically unsafe in a way I haven’t since my VA days. We managed the situation clinically, but that combination—acute suicidality plus immediate access to a gun in the parking lot—was exactly the kind of foreseeable risk that keeps me up at night.

More recently, in LL-25 we’ve been running sessions with a high-conflict custody case. The father has a documented history of brandishing a firearm during a domestic dispute. When both parents are in LL-W2, with siblings and other families present, the emotional temperature is high. The idea that he could be lawfully carrying while we’re giving feedback about parenting capacity is, frankly, terrifying.

I also continue to think about a 2023 incident at the HBSC 110 reception desk, when an agitated community client threatened to “come back with a gun.” After that, I made an informal call to a contact at UHPD just to clarify how much authority I had to ask someone to leave or initiate a welfare check without automatically generating a formal report. It highlighted for me how much gray area there is for front-line clinical staff.

All of this is happening while I’m trying to protect trainees—one second-year was so shaken by a crisis in LL-21 that they started carrying a concealed handgun off-campus just to walk to their car. That’s not the training environment we want.

Happy to talk more, but I wanted you to have concrete context for why I’m pushing this.

Best,
Melissa

Dr. Melissa A. Rodriguez
Director, Psychological Services Clinic
Department of Psychology, UH

metadata.subject: Context for HBSC clinic exclusion zone discussions

artifacts.content:
Event: Crisis & Safety Debrief with Trainees – HBSC LL-25

Time: Monday, 2025-04-07, 8:30–10:00 a.m.
Location: LL-25 (Group Therapy Room), Health and Behavioral Sciences Center
Attendees: Melissa; 1st- and 2nd-year clinic trainees

Agenda/Notes:

1. Review recent high-risk episodes in the Psychological Services Clinic
- Revisit the late-2024 LL-18 near-hospitalization case (student with acute suicidal ideation who mentioned having a handgun in his car outside HBSC). Use as a teaching example for risk assessment, safety planning, and when to involve UHPD.
- Discuss the ongoing high-conflict custody case in LL-25, including the non-custodial father’s prior incident of brandishing a firearm during a domestic dispute and how that history affects safety planning in LL-W2 and HBSC 110.
- Briefly reference the 2023 HBSC 110 reception incident (“come back with a gun” threat) and how front desk staff and trainees should respond if they hear similar statements.

2. Trainee well-being
- Normalize emotional reactions after crisis sessions. Without naming names, mention that in 2024 a second-year trainee was so shaken after a crisis in LL-21 that they broke down in supervision and later disclosed they had started carrying a concealed handgun off-campus because they felt unsafe walking to their car after late nights in the building.
- Emphasize use of supervision, peer consultation, and personal therapy as needed (model by acknowledging I’ve sought my own therapy near the Med Center for burnout/anxiety related to repeated crises in LL-10–24).

3. Policy and environment
- Explain where proposed campus carry exclusion signage would go (HBSC 110 entrance, LL elevator lobby, stairwell exit to clinic corridor) and why.
- Invite questions about when to call UHPD vs. when to manage in-session.

Reminder to self: leave 10 minutes at the end just to check in with each trainee one-on-one about how they’re actually doing.

Hi Carla,

Finally answering your “how are you really?” text with something more than a thumbs-up.

The honest version: I’m tired, but not in the catastrophic way I was in 2023 before I started seeing my own therapist over by the Texas Medical Center. You remember—three near-hospitalizations in those basement rooms (LL-10 through LL-24), the community client at HBSC 110 who threatened to “come back with a gun,” and I was waking up at 3 a.m. replaying every safety decision. Weekly therapy since mid-2023 has taken the sharpest edge off the burnout and anxiety, though I still have weeks I feel like a hypocrite telling trainees to practice self-care.

The clinic itself is a pressure cooker. We had that LL-18 case in late 2024 where the student said he didn’t know why he kept going and then mentioned a handgun in his car outside HBSC. In LL-25 there’s the custody family with the dad who once brandished a firearm during a domestic fight. And one of my second-years dissolved in tears after a crisis in LL-21 and admitted they’d started carrying a concealed handgun off-campus just to feel safe walking to their car at night. That one broke my heart.

On top of that, I’m quietly coordinating with you and the CAPS folks about how we phrase the bilingual firearm exclusion notices for HBSC 110 and the LL elevator lobby. I keep thinking about a couple of Latinx families in high-conflict cases who are already terrified of relatives with guns. I don’t want the Spanish signage to feel like it’s aimed at them or to draw attention in a way that puts them at more risk.

Home is the usual circus—Elena’s piano, Mateo’s soccer, Carlos’s robotics kids. The new wrinkle is my dad. His orthopedist in Sugar Land told him earlier this year he shouldn’t be driving into Houston anymore because of his knees and slowed reaction time. He’s stubborn, but it makes me more aware that if something serious happened on campus, my parents couldn’t just rush over.

Anyway, this is long. Can we still do our Friday lunch this week? I really need that hour to decompress.

Abrazos,
Melissa

Thread with Ana (Messages)

2025-01-22 8:14 p.m.

Ana: How’s my favorite hermana? You sounded wiped on Sunday.

Me: Still wiped lol. Long day in the HBSC basement.

Ana: What happened?

Me: Nothing dramatic today, just the usual high-acuity mix. But it sits on top of all the stuff from last year.

Me: I keep thinking about that LL-18 session in November – suicidal UH student, walked out saying he didn’t know why he kept going, then casually mentions he has a gun in his car outside the Health and Behavioral Sciences Center. Senior trainee and I looked at each other like, “this is exactly why we need an exclusion zone.”

Ana: Jesus. That’s terrifying.

Me: And then there’s the LL-25 custody case with the dad who once pulled a gun in a fight at home. When both parents are in the LL-W2 waiting area with the kids, my stomach’s in knots.

Ana: I don’t know how you do this.

Me: Therapy helps. I’ve been going to that woman near the Med Center since mid-2023, remember? After the third near-hospitalization and the guy at HBSC 110 who threatened to “come back with a gun,” I just couldn’t pretend I was fine.

Ana: I’m glad you kept going. You sound more grounded than you did back then.

Me: Some days. Other days I worry that pushing so hard to make the HBSC clinic a campus carry exclusion zone is going to get me labeled as the “overreactive Latina professor who’s anti-gun.” Texas politics + being a Mexican American woman = fun combo.

Ana: Ugh. If anyone says that, send them to me.

Me: Lol. On top of it all, Dad’s doctor in Sugar Land told him this month he really shouldn’t be driving into Houston anymore because of his knees and slow reflexes. He told me, “I’ll be fine,” but it just adds another layer of worry.

Ana: We’ll tag-team them on the downsizing convo when you come to SA.

Me: Deal. I’m going to bed. Love you.

Ana: Love you too.

To: [CAPS colleague]
From: marodriguez@uh.edu
Date: 2025-07-02
Subject: Bilingual signage language – quick gut check

Hey,

I’m tweaking the draft language for the bilingual firearm exclusion signs for HBSC 110 and the LL elevator lobby and wanted your eyes on it before I send anything up the chain.

We’re planning the standard Texas-required wording in English and Spanish, plus a short line like “Psychological Services Clinic – Mental Health Treatment Area” so people understand this isn’t about classrooms. My only hesitation is how it will land with a few of our current Latinx families.

We’ve got at least two high-conflict cases right now where relatives have used guns to intimidate or threaten. One mom told me she’s terrified that if certain family members realize the clinic is trying to limit firearm access around LL-W1/W2 and HBSC 110, it could be seen as a challenge and escalate things. I don’t want the Spanish text to feel like it’s aimed at them or to make them more visible in the waiting room.

At the same time, the broader context hasn’t changed: the LL-18 student last November who walked out of session talking about not wanting to live and then mentioned having a handgun in his car outside HBSC; the LL-25 custody dad with a history of brandishing a gun; the community client at HBSC 110 who threatened to “come back with a gun.” And then the second-year who broke down after the LL-21 crisis and told me they’d started carrying a concealed handgun off-campus to feel safer walking to their car. All of that is why we’re doing this.

I’m also aware of my own stuff around this. As a Mexican American woman pushing hard on a gun-related policy in Texas, I’m already bracing for the “emotional Latina making everything political” stereotype.

Can you skim the attached wording and tell me if anything in the Spanish reads as stigmatizing or unnecessarily alarmist? Maybe we can soften the explanatory line while keeping the legal language intact.

Thanks,
M.

metadata.subject: Bilingual signage language – quick gut check
